title: Bezos strikes $11bn satellite deal to rival Musk
detail:
Bezos strikes $11bn satellite deal to rival Musk
Matthew Field
Tue, April 14, 2026 at 9:51 AM MST 3 min read
AMZN
GSAT
Explore stocks on Coinbase
Trading disclosure
Jeff Bezos unveiled Amazon’s satellite efforts in 2019, two years before stepping down as chief executive - Jonathan Newton/The Washington Post via Getty Images

Jeff Bezos’s Amazon has struck an $11.5bn (£8.5bn) deal to acquire a major satellite operator as it seeks to challenge Elon Musk’s Starlink.

The American tech giant confirmed a deal on Tuesday to buy US-based Globalstar, tapping the company’s network of communications satellites to beam down mobile phone signals from space.

As part of the deal, the business will merge Globalstar’s operations with its own satellite constellation division, called Amazon Leo.

The subsidiary has been developed by Amazon to provide internet data from orbit and has so far launched more than 240 satellites out of a planned 3,000.

Panos Panay, senior vice-president at Amazon, said the deal would allow the tech giant to work with mobile operators to “extend coverage to every corner of the planet”.

The deal sets up Amazon to offer smartphone signals from space by utilising Globalstar’s broad mobile spectrum rights.

This will put the business in direct competition with Mr Musk’s Starlink, which has gained popularity by providing high-speed connectivity to smartphones.

Amazon said it planned to launch mobile services from 2028, with enough capacity to support “hundreds of millions” of customers.

It also confirmed it had reached an agreement with Apple to power its emergency SOS messaging features on iPhone and Apple Watch.

Growing competition

Globalstar previously offered coverage for emergency satellite phones and connectivity for disaster zones. Apple invested more than $1bn in Globalstar in 2024 as part of an agreement to power satellite services and SOS alerts on its iPhones.

Mr Bezos unveiled Amazon’s satellite efforts in 2019, two years before stepping down as chief executive. The Amazon billionaire also owns Blue Origin, a rocket business that competes with SpaceX.

Mr Musk has repeatedly mocked Mr Bezos by calling him a “copycat” because of his space plans.

Amazon started launching its first clusters of satellites last year and now has hundreds in orbit. However, it is still far behind Mr Musk’s Starlink, which has more than 10,000 spacecraft in low Earth orbit, about 300 miles above the planet.

Analysts have estimated Amazon’s Leo network could cost as much as $20bn, even as Andy Jassy, its current chief executive, seeks to rein in spending with thousands of job cuts and redirect investment towards AI.

The takeover is expected to close in 2027 and will probably be scrutinised by global regulators. Industry sources also expect the deal to face stiff resistance from Mr Musk’s business.

Globalstar, which was founded in 1991, had been courting potential suitors for months and last year reportedly held abortive talks with SpaceX about a possible sale.
